item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Much like SCP-55, it's the implications of what an SCP like this means (or what it can do) that serve to unnerve me on some fundamental and primal level that I'm not sure I can adequately explain. There's such an inherent wrongness that comes from reading the second report, that makes me greatly excited for what may come next with the Department of Misinformation. I love the Anti-Memetic tales so much, and this article definitely captures some of that feeling for me. This is one that will be itching the back of my mind for awhile.
A very renaång +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm having a hard time understanding how the language used by Project Blah members came into being. I'm assuming, for one, that all the unusual words in this article are made up by the author, and not drawn from real isolated languages. So, is this a case where Blah initiates who perceive the object are making spontaneous observations like "oh, it's definitely [çeartuuate]!" and others are nodding along, like these words are just coming to them naturally as descriptors for its qualities? Or is it more along the lines of these being agreed upon words, created intentionally to describe qualities that no satisfactory words existed for previously? The fact that the new words are not easy to pronounce, and draw symbols from a number of different alphabets seems to suggest the former, unless the anomaly actually affects the ability of languages to describe it (like, by making simpler neologisms seem wrong or inadequate as descriptors), rather than just being innately difficult to describe. Or, I guess a third option, this being specifically DoMc, is the difficulty of pronouncing the words part of the containment? Like, would substituting simpler neologisms allow the anomaly to harm those attempting to describe it, or somehow breach containment through the use of language, as a sort of infectious concept? I dunno, I feel like there's a very interesting idea in here but I'm just a bit lost trying to parse it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is the idea that the three most compatible languages are all relatively isolated from other language families? If so, I think you might mean "Vedda". If, instead, you mean the language the Vedas are written in, "Vedic Sanskrit" is a more accepted term.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1 for

"conventionally indeterminate size, weight, composition, etc."

It does happen that often, doesn't it.

"Oh, and also we can't tell its size, weight, and so on. Pretty normal for our department."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't think it's normal, but brains are weird. I don't think you should be worried about it. And anyway, the skip said posuɕkis can be triggered non-anomalously :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Posuɕkis can be approximately described as a temporary state, during which a person and/or sapient being thinks without the use of words

Wait, isnt that normal? This is how like. 90% of my thoughts are.

+1 btw.. the DoMc language felt like jargon crossed with a brain aneurysm

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wonder if Operation Blah would help people refer to Me as anything other than a toaster, or with any of the other anti-memes.

item.title: Re: The Department of Miscommunications Debuts
item.content:encoded: Mmmm y'know we actually have an object class for objects that the foundation deals with like anomalies but are otherwise completely explained ;) ("Godel" from 4555, this is EXACTLY the kind of thing I had in mind when I thought it up! Not that you have to use it just, this kind of stuff gets me really pumped!!!).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So, people have a point that this doesn't have a lot of stakes to it, but personally I don't see what's stopping anyone from writing a tale to expand on how this article came to be, right? I mean, there are entire hubs dedicated to specific departments inside the Foundation, right? (I mean, I know there is for the Anti-Memetics division, at least). Why not make that happen here?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The moment where it switched into the DoMC's language had me grinning hard. This was real clever, and while like Kirby said this doesn't have much stakes to it, the ideas and their execution more than made up for that for me.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I admit that I have written a practically stakesless story. It's more about [spoilers] the Department of Miscommunications being really sneaky, and hopefully having the execution be so underplayed in the article that the reader gets to the end, sits for a second, and then goes "…hey wait a minute". So, that's a result of the story I wanted to tell, and the way I wanted to tell it. Totally fair that it didn't work for you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, I think the concept is clever. I like the way this plays with language - however I think that by setting the SCP article after the Foundation has figured out how to deal with it limits the story immensely. Like, I would've loved to see the foundation flounder for a bit as they try to think of how the hell to deal with this thing. But instead what you have here is pretty purely concept, but the concept itself does not have much of a sense of escalation. Like, there's a change between part 1 and part 2, but it doesn't feel like a noticeable escalation, if that makes sense.

I'm going to have to novote because while I find the concept clever, the article itself didn't really do much for me.

item.title: The Department of Miscommunications Debuts
item.content:encoded:
Thank you to FloppyPhoenix, MaliceAforethought, and Mortos for critiquing this. Also, huge thank you to MaliceAforethought for making all of the "nonsense" words. And extra, extra special thanks to both MaliceAforethought and Mortos for being part of the brainstorming session that made this happen.

So, welcome to the debut of DoMc, or the Department of Miscommunications. Yes, you heard that right. This is a debut. Expect some future installments, perhaps from Mortos and MaliceAforethought…

But enough about the stuff surrounding the article, more about the article itself! This came about because I hadn't written and posted for two months (the Roget collab was mostly Roget), and I was itching to get something out. Cue a well timed brainstorming session started by Mortos talking about the incomprehensible and difficult to communicate. We get to talking, MaliceAforethought shows up, the Department of Miscommunications comes to be, and we decide we'll each write at least one article about it (at our leisure). Well I was damn excited, and I pumped this out pretty fast once I started on it. I wanted to write about a successful attempt, on the Foundation's part, to fully understand an anomaly. In fact, one could argue that since they have figured out how to neutralize its property (communicate about it) and have researched its byproduct (thinking without words), it is non-anomalous. But, that's precisely where some intra-Foundational politics come into play.

Once I had them succeed, I knew I needed a bit more… so why not flesh out the Department of Miscommunications on the way? And here we are. It also was my goal to make another article like #MassaTruthetts, in that it was entirely concept driven and did not have a drop of dialogue, exploration log, or anything other than dry summation of events. I quite enjoy writing stuff like that, so I wanted to do it again. Here's to hoping it worked!

Cheers, and I'm glad to be back.

EDIT: I feel the need to justify why I gave it only two tags. Simply because that is so ridiculously low. The only two tags, beyond the page tags (scp and esoteric-class), that I could consider were "language", "mind-affecting", and "observational". Here's why none of those work.

In case you care...
...here's my explanation.

language - SCP imparts or exhibits an anomalous language.

Well, while this has a lot to do with language, the SCP itself doesn't do this. The Foundation (or DoMc) made a whole operation that involved language to get around the SCP's effects, but the SCP itself has actually nothing to do with anomalous language. So, that's that tag out the window.

mind-affecting - SCP affects the thinking of subjects, which can include behavior and overall intelligence.

Well, it doesn't. It doesn't affect behavior or overall intelligence. It affects your thinking in just the same way that an apple affects your thinking — by looking at it, you are now processing the properties of the apple. The only difference here is that those properties aren't attached to any words in any terrestrial language — but we know that that's not too large a hurdle, as we can make words and attach those qualities to those words, which negates that effect of its, and proves it's not inherently mind affecting.

observational - SCP has properties that are triggered or suppressed when directly or indirectly observed.

Well, sure, when you observe it, you think about it, and thinking about it causes weird things… but we just went over why those weird things aren't anomalously mind affecting, which negates the observational property, too. It has all the properties of a normal object, it is only that we don't have words for the specific properties it possesses. Otherwise, it interacts with our brains like anything else.

For further evidence, we are told that posuɕkis is not a property indigenous to SCP-4467 (don't question my word choice, you know what I mean so it worked). With discipline, it can be activated and deactivated at will, and we are given this snippet:

[…] but [it] is currently poorly researched due to being, beyond SCP-4467, rarely achievable.

Note "rarely". It is not unachievable beyond SCP-4467, just rarely. So. Yeah. That's why my skip only has two tags. Nothing else even slightly applies.

Call me the winner of Bryx's tag free SCP challenge, because I think I just won.

EDIT: This ended up on a podcast and I didn't even know until now! Thanks, pxdnbluesoul!
